skill.name: x-publish
skill.description: Prepare tweets and threads as X draft posts using browser automation and a local clipboard helper. The skill stages content for manual review and does not intentionally publish posts automatically.
skill.summary: Stage tweets and threads as X drafts for manual review.
skill.author: kangarooking

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged subprocess use, filesystem access, hardcoded URLs, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found no evidence of malicious intent, prompt injection, weak cryptography, or command injection; the subprocess calls use fixed clipboard commands without shell interpolation. The skill still has medium risk because it reads user-selected files into the system clipboard and automates a logged-in X browser session to stage social media drafts.

medium_findings.title: User-Selected File Content Copied to System Clipboard
medium_findings.description: The helper accepts a --file path, reads that file, and copies its contents to the system clipboard. This is legitimate for prepared tweet text, but it can expose sensitive local file contents if an agent or user supplies the wrong path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly reads a caller-supplied file path and places the contents on the clipboard. There is no evidence of automatic network exfiltration, so the risk is contextual rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Browser Automation for X Draft Creation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs browser automation to open X compose pages, paste content, and save drafts in the user's logged-in account. It states that posts must not be auto-published, but the workflow still operates in a sensitive authenticated social media session.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions clearly target a logged-in X compose workflow and include explicit draft-saving steps. The safeguard against auto-publishing lowers severity but does not remove the account-action risk.

low_findings.title: Fixed Clipboard Subprocess Commands
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Python subprocess.Popen on Linux. The command names and arguments are hardcoded for xclip and xsel, stdin receives only tweet text, and shell=True is not used, so this is not command injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls use fixed argument lists and pass user content through stdin. This makes the static finding real subprocess use but a false positive for command injection.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Executable Backticks
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged many SKILL.md code fences as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are Markdown workflow examples and browser automation steps, not executable code embedded in the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content appears inside Markdown fences and examples. It describes intended usage but is not itself a hidden execution mechanism.

low_findings.title: Expected Hardcoded X URLs
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged hardcoded URLs. The URLs point to x.com compose and draft pages, which match the stated purpose of staging X drafts and do not indicate unknown third-party exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All reviewed URLs are on x.com and align with the skill description. No unrelated webhook, paste, or data collection endpoint was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm blockers, but the cited lines are frontmatter text, table formatting, and argparse setup. No cryptographic operation was found at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain no hashing, encryption, or security-sensitive crypto API. This is a strong false positive based on the reviewed file contents.

low_findings.title: Temporary File Examples Are User-Controlled Workflow Storage
low_findings.description: The instructions show writing tweet text to /tmp/tweet.txt before copying it to the clipboard. This is a common workflow pattern, but users should avoid placing sensitive unpublished content in shared temporary paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temporary path use is explicit and limited to user-provided content examples. It is a minor data exposure concern on shared systems, not evidence of malware.

dangerous_patterns.title: Clipboard Modification
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper writes content to the operating system clipboard across macOS, Windows, and Linux. Clipboard state may be visible to other local applications.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The implementation is clearly designed to modify the system clipboard. This is expected for the skill but remains a sensitive local action.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Read
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper can read a caller-supplied local file and copy its full contents to the clipboard.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The --file option is explicit and directly read with open(). The risk depends on what path the user or agent provides.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Automation Against Authenticated Web Account
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow uses browser navigation and clicks in X compose pages while the user is logged in.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill requires Playwright MCP and a logged-in X account, then issues compose-page actions. The documented draft-only control reduces but does not eliminate account-action risk.

content.user_title: Stage X Drafts from Prepared Posts
content.value_statement: Posting drafts to X can be slow when content is prepared outside the browser. This skill copies prepared text and uses browser automation to stage tweets or threads as drafts for manual review.
actual_capabilities: Copies tweet text to the system clipboard from direct input, stdin, or a local file.
Guides browser automation to open the X compose page.
Stages a single tweet draft with pasted content.
Stages multi-post thread drafts by adding additional posts.
Saves the compose window as a draft instead of intentionally publishing.
Reports the X drafts page for final manual review.
limitations: Requires the user to already be logged in to X in the browser session.
Requires Playwright MCP or similar browser automation support.
Depends on local clipboard access and platform-specific clipboard tools.
Does not verify final X UI changes beyond the documented workflow.

use_cases.title: Save a polished post draft
use_cases.description: Move a reviewed short post into X drafts without manually copying and opening the compose page.

use_cases.title: Prepare a launch thread
use_cases.description: Stage multiple numbered posts as one thread draft for final approval before publication.

use_cases.title: Review generated content in X
use_cases.description: Place assistant-written content into the native X draft interface so a human can inspect it before posting.

prompt_templates.title: Draft one post
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single X draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Use x-publish to stage this as an X draft: [paste the post text]. Save it only as a draft.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a short thread
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for multiple prepared posts
prompt_templates.prompt: Use x-publish to stage this thread as an X draft. Keep each numbered section as a separate post and do not publish it.

prompt_templates.title: Draft from a local text file
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request using prepared local content
prompt_templates.prompt: Use x-publish with the prepared text file at [path]. Confirm the content length, copy it to the clipboard, and save it as an X draft.

prompt_templates.title: Stage and verify a campaign thread
prompt_templates.prompt: Use x-publish to stage this campaign thread. Check each post length, preserve the post order, save the thread as a draft, and report the draft review link.

output_examples.input: Stage this one-sentence product update as an X draft.
output_examples.output: The post was copied to the clipboard, pasted into X compose, and saved as a draft. Review it in X drafts before publishing.

output_examples.input: Stage a three-part launch thread from prepared content.
output: The thread was split into three posts.
Each post was added to the X compose thread.
The thread was saved as a draft for manual review.

output_examples.input: Use the prepared tweet file and save it to drafts.
output_examples.output: The file content was copied to the clipboard and staged in X compose. The draft was saved for final review.

best_practices: Review tweet text and character counts before starting browser automation.
Use draft-only mode and manually inspect the final X draft before publishing.
Use dedicated temporary files that contain only the intended post text.
anti_patterns: Do not point the file option at private notes, secrets, or unrelated documents.
Do not ask the skill to publish directly without human review.
Do not run the workflow in a browser session for the wrong X account.

faq.question: Does this skill publish posts automatically?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow saves content as an X draft for manual review.

faq.question: What account does it use?
faq.answer: It uses the X account already logged in within the browser automation session.

faq.question: Can it create threads?
faq.answer: Yes. It can stage multiple prepared posts as a thread draft.

faq.question: Why does it need clipboard access?
faq.answer: The helper copies prepared text so browser automation can paste it into X compose.

faq.question: Can it read from a file?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper can read a user-provided text file and copy that content to the clipboard.

faq.question: What should users check before using it?
faq.answer: Users should confirm the X account, content, file path, and draft status before any manual publication.
